summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Application for a property settlement – De Facto relationship – Allegations of family violence – Allegations that respondent has retained substantial monies and assets – Limited tangible property pool – Contributions – Post-separation contributions – Adjustments pursuant to s 90SM(5) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) and consideration of reality testing those adjustments against limited value of property

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

McGUIRE J:

APPLICATION

These are property proceedings between parties to a de facto relationship with a number of credit and factual complexities but with a limited current property pool agreed as follows:

Property

1.	Monies held in trust by Romer Maud Solicitors	$235,650.91
2.	Property situated at 1B Street, Suburb C (registered in the applicant’s name) (agreed value)	$630,000.00
Total property	$865,650.91

Liabilities

1.	Mortgage NAB – 1B Street, Suburb C	$552,481.00
2.	Outstanding council rates	$6,698.10
3.	Outstanding body corporate	$25,335.00
Total Liabilities	$584,514.10

	

Total net property pool	$281,136.81
Importantly, however, and whilst the current iteration of s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) dictates that the Court’s finding of the contents of the property pool relates only to “current assets”, Ms Dilay, (“the applicant”) here argues that Mr Shahrukh (“the respondent”) remains in possession of some assets and notably Motor Vehicle 1. She argues accordingly that the Court should include that vehicle in the property pool set out above.
The applicant seeks orders effectively giving her 100 per cent of the property. She does so on the basis of superior contributions with significant adjustments under s 90SM(4) and (5) of the Act where she has the care and financial responsibility for the two children of the relationship; where she argues from a history of family violence perpetrated by the respondent; and her weighty claims that the respondent has retained substantial monies and assets from the complex history of financial transactions conducted by him highlighted by the purchase and sale of properties and expensive motor vehicles. Suffice to say that the applicant argues the respondent has retained assets which far outweigh and out value those comprised in the property pool. It is proper to note, and as properly conceded by counsel for the applicant, that the current interpretation of recent amendments to s 79 of the Act does not permit “add-backs” or “notional assets” into a property pool.[1]
Where the forensic history and tracing exercise here is a difficult one in the extreme and of questionable utility given the minimal value of the property pool, such an exercise was made more onerous by the respondent being self-represented for all but the last day of the trial and where the respondent, having discharged his s 102NA appointed lawyer in the week before the trial, had drafted his own affidavit only in the two days prior to the trial, and without criticism of the respondent, it is reasonable to say that the content of his affidavit was generally of little assistance to the Court in its determination.
Further, also pursuant to s 102NA, the respondent was prohibited from cross-examining the applicant but received some assistance from the Bench asking the applicant questions in an effort to clarify the cases of both the applicant and respondent and in this sense to put the respondent’s case to the applicant.
To this end, the Court is extremely grateful to Mr Faust of counsel appearing for the respondent on the last day of the trial and for final submissions. Although the evidence was effectively completed by the time of Mr Faust’s appearance, his involvement served to calm to a large degree the respondent in his propensity for excitable and combative frequent interjection and also to provide some balance to the case argued by the respondent and the orders he sought.
In his Outline of Case document the respondent naïvely and/or unreasonably sought 100 per cent of the property pool. A reading of that document, however, and many of the gratuitous comments made by the respondent during the trial, highly suggests his initiative in these proceedings being, at least in part, to conduct the financial trial as a form of de facto re-run of the parenting proceedings previously settled by consent orders between the parties.
By the time of final submissions, and with the timely intervention and balance of his counsel, I understood the respondent to be conceding the applicant to retain her Suburb C property but, on my understanding, that he benefit either totally or in part from the other major asset being the cash funds held in trust.

CHRONOLOGY

The applicant is 41 years of age. The respondent is 39 years of age.
The applicant works casually in a food service. The respondent is currently in the retail sector. He has historically operated his own business.
There are two children of the relationship being X born in 2018 (aged seven years) and Y born in 2021 (aged four years).
The children live with the applicant pursuant to final parenting orders made 8 February 2020. Those orders also provide for the children to spend time with the respondent by agreement between the parties. No time has taken place since March 2024 where the applicant alleges continuing family violence by the respondent. The applicant has on at least three separate occasions been required to return to the Court to obtain recovery orders following the respondent taking the children into his possession.
The respondent pays no child support to the applicant for the children.
The applicant’s trial affidavit is replete with a history of family violence allegations against the respondent being violence of various types. She has made complaints to the police. She has obtained family violence intervention orders. The respondent has been charged criminally and for breaches of the order. The applicant has at times taken refuge in a women’s shelter.
The respondent also makes allegations of family violence against the applicant.
The parties commenced a relationship in October 2009. They commenced cohabitation in January 2010. A first separation occurred in December 2019. The applicant says there was a short reconciliation in August 2021, but that separation finally occurred in October 2021.
In 2011 the respondent commenced his own business under a corporate entity “H Pty Ltd”.
In June 2012 the parties purchased vacant land at D Street, Suburb E (“the [Suburb E] property”) for $219,000 with a 5 per cent deposit and the remainder by mortgage loan.
In late 2013 the applicant purchased Property 1B Street, Suburb C (“[Property 1B Street]”). The title has at all times been registered in her sole name. The purchase costs were $394,110.
Contemporaneously the respondent purchased, in his sole name, the 2B Street, [2B Street] (“[2B Street]”) with the assistance of a mortgage.
Construction of a townhouse residence was completed on 1B Street in 2014 and the parties moved into that property. The property at 2B Street was at all relevant times tenanted.
In May 2015 the “J Discretionary Trust” was established with H Pty Ltd being the trustee company.
In September 2015 a hospitality business “[K Business]” was purchased for $200,000.
H Pty Ltd was deregistered in August 2017 and in November 2017 the parties became directors and shareholders of the L Pty Ltd.
In 2018 the respondent created an NAB portfolio facility effectively consolidating the mortgages of the two Suburb C properties and the Suburb E property together with other credit facilities.
In 2018 the respondent purchased Motor Vehicle 1 registered in the name of “J Pty Ltd”.
In late 2018 J Pty Ltd sold “[K Business]” for $200,000.
In 2019 the parties purchased a property at F Street, suburb G (“the [Suburb G] property”) in the name of L Pty Ltd for $1,700,000. The purchase was funded through savings and a mortgage of $1,090,827 with M Financial Services.
The parties first separated in December 2019.
In May 2020 Child Protection obtained a warrant to recover the child X from the respondent and an Interim Accommodation Order was made in favour of the applicant.
Child Protection records one month later show a confirmation in respect of threats by the respondent to kill the applicant and the applicant obtaining an FVIO against the respondent.
On 7 September 2020 the applicant commenced family law proceedings such discontinued in October 2020 following a reconciliation but not of cohabitation.
In 2021 the respondent was convicted of persistent contravention of the Family Violence Order, contravention of an interim FVIO, and of bail conditions.
In mid-2021 the respondent’s sale of property 2B Street was settled where after payment of the $206,832 towards the joint NAB portfolio loan, the respondent retained $287,722.
In mid-2021 the respondent purchased Motor Vehicle 3.
In August 2021 the parties resumed cohabitation.
In September 2021 the applicant says that she sold Motor Vehicle 2 on representations by the respondent that she would have Motor Vehicle 1.
The parties separated finally in October 2021 after the applicant alleged an assault on her by the respondent.
In 2022 the respondent purchased Motor Vehicle 4.
In April 2022 the applicant alleges further assaults at the hands of the respondent and that he over held X. She obtained an FVIO against the respondent.
In May 2022 the applicant commenced these proceedings on the basis of the respondent withholding X.
A recovery order for X was made on the applicant’s application on 7 May 2022.
On 1 July 2022 an interim order was made permitting only limited supervised time of the older time X with the respondent.
On 17 August 2022 orders were made for the respondent to hand the Motor Vehicle 1 to the applicant. The respondent has never complied with that order.
On 20 September 2022 a further order was made for the respondent to hand Motor Vehicle 1 to the applicant and to register the vehicle. The respondent has not complied with this order.
On 16 November 2022 an interim order was made inter-alia for the respondent to pay $130,000 into the applicant’s lawyers trust account with $20,000 for expert witness expenses and the remainder of $110,000 “to be characterised at trial”. The respondent was to refinance the mortgage security over the applicant’s home at property 1B Street. Orders were made for the sale of the Suburb G property in default.
On 6 January 2023 the mortgage in respect of the Suburb G property was in arrears of $76,096.
In January 2023 the applicant alleges the presence of the respondent at her home and damage to the property. She and the children then spent nine months in safe accommodation at a women’s refuge.
In March 2020 the selling agents advised the respondent still to be living in the Suburb G property with significant damage to that property.
In early 2023 the Suburb E property sold for $581,000 with orders for the applicant to be appointed trustee for the sale with the balance funds to be held in trust.
In July 2023 the sale of the Suburb E property settled but with the respondent, in breach of court orders, redirecting the deposit of $17,074 to himself.
In December 2023 the respondent re-registered Motor Vehicle 1 still in his possession but contrary to extant orders for it to be handed to the applicant.
On 8 February 2024 final parenting orders were made by consent between the parties but absent the consent of the ICL providing inter-alia for time between the children and respondent by agreement between the parents.
The children last spent time with the respondent on 3 March 2024.
The sale of the Suburb G property settled in early 2024 with a sale price of $1,800,000 but with the selling agent noting significant damage to the property.
On 10 March 2024 the respondent again took possession of the child X.
On 9 May 2024 the respondent again took possession of X from her school utilising a fake parenting plan. A further recovery order was issued on 16 May 2024 in favour of the applicant.
In late 2024 the respondent was charged with eight counts of breaches of an FIVO; two counts of persistent contravention of an FIVO; and make and utilise a false document.
In early 2025 the respondent was charged with two further breaches an FIVO.
On 28 April 2025, on the first day of the trial, final parenting orders were made by consent and property issues were adjourned to 13 October 2025 with orders for the respondent to make full disclosure and for a valuation of the applicant’s property at property 1, B Street.
Later in 2025 the respondent was convicted of multiple family violence related offences.
On 29 August 2025 the respondent ceased his relationship with his lawyers.
The trial of this matter commenced on 13 October 2025. The respondent was self-represented. He prepared his affidavit only days before the trial. The applicant was also represented pursuant to s 102NA of the Act.

THE RELEVANT LAW

Section 90SM of the Act provides for the alteration of property interests between parties to a de facto relationship where the Court enjoys a broad discretion but subject to the provisions of the statute itself.
The process for the Court is to firstly determine the property pool and attribute value as at the date of the trial. Property consists of both assets and liabilities. Superannuation may be treated as property although not strictly crystallised as a tangible asset. The parties here hold no superannuation entitlements of significant value.
The Court is to independently consider at s 90SM(2) whether it be just and equitable, in all the circumstances, to make any order altering the property interests of the parties. Such a consideration is to be a distinct one circumstantially to the facts of the case and not to be simply conflated with the considerations of contributions at s 90SM(3).
Again, the current understanding of s 90SM(3) is that the Court is to consider the “existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties”. Relevant to this case, the Court is no longer able to include “notional assets” or “add-backs” where such matters must now be dealt with under s 90SM(5) in consideration of adjustments after contributions.
It is proper to observe and find at this stage that it is just and equitable for the Court to move to consider an alteration of the parties’ property interest. It is clear that the relationship has ended. There are assets held jointly on behalf of the parties in the form of monies on trust. There are two children of the relationship. Without conflating the consideration of contributions, such a consideration remains relevant to the justice and equity determination at 90SM(2).[2]
After identifying the property pool and determining that it is just and equitable to enter into a consideration of altering the interests in that pool, the Court turns to consider the contributions by or on behalf of the parties to the property pool. Those contributions may be of a direct or indirect financial type. They may be of a non-financial type including as homemaker and parent.
Significant recent amendments to the Act at s 90SM(4)(ca) mandates the Court to consider:
the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the de facto relationship has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the de facto relationship to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c).
After making findings as to contributions, the Court turns at s 90SM(5) to consider and determine whether it be just and equitable to make any further adjustment to one or other of the parties in respect of the numerous matters set out at that subsection. Among the relevant factors to consider in respect of this matter are the following:
(a) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the de facto relationship (the subject de facto relationship) has subjected or exposed the other party, on the current and future circumstances of the other party, including on any of the matters mentioned elsewhere in this subsection;

(b) the age and state of health of each of the parties to the subject de facto relationship;

(c) the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties to the subject de facto relationship and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment;

(d) the effect of any material wastage, caused intentionally or recklessly by a party to the subject de facto relationship, of property or financial...;

...
(f) the extent to which either party to the subject de facto relationship has the care of a child of the de facto relationship who has not attained the age of 18 years, including the need of either party to provide appropriate housing for such a child;

(g) commitments of each of the parties to the subject de facto relationship that are necessary to enable the party to support themselves and any child or other person that the party has a duty to maintain;

...
(o) the duration of the subject de facto relationship and the extent to which it has affected the earning capacity of each party to the subject de facto relationship;
...
(t) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the subject de facto relationship is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the subject de facto relationship;
...

FINDINGS AND CONCLUSIONS

Where numerous issues of disputed fact remain prevalent in this matter, the credit of the parties is a significant issue. Generally, where, issues of credit arise, I prefer the evidence of the applicant over that of the respondent.
The respondent was vocal, combative, and argumentative in his evidence in both written and oral form. He was frequently unresponsive and argumentative in the witness box. Much of his evidence I find to be unconvincing when in response and when challenged. He did not adduce evidence or corroboration when such might reasonably have been available. His denials of family violence were unpersuasive and sit uncomfortably with the highly particularised evidence of the applicant corroborated by frequent successful applications for family violence orders and successful prosecutions for breaches of those orders together with other factors such as the applicant and children being required to take safe housing in a women’s refuge for a period of nine months when the applicant otherwise had her own home available to her.
I also had the advantage of seeing and hearing the applicant give her evidence. Whilst she was open for proper criticism from the respondent as to her method of adducing evidence of the quotations in respect of repairs to her property, I found the applicant to generally give her evidence in a clear, informed and consistent fashion. Unlike the respondent, she was able to make admissions against interest where appropriate. Her evidence was highly particularised. Perhaps with no direct fault to the respondent, much of the applicant’s evidence was unchallenged by reason of the s 102NA order made for the benefit of the respondent but where he appeared at the trial initially unrepresented. As unfortunate as that circumstance might be, the fact is that much of the applicant’s evidence remained unchallenged despite the Bench itself entering the arena to put the respondent’s case to the applicant and challenge some of the applicant’s evidence.
The asset pool is agreed as set out above in these reasons save and except for the issue of the existence of Motor Vehicle 1.
The applicant says that the respondent retains possession or control of that vehicle. The respondent denies this assertion. On the balance of probabilities I find that the respondent does retain possession or control of that vehicle and that it should, therefore, be included in the property pool.
Relevantly, an order was made by this Court on 20 September 2022 followed by a similar order being that the respondent return that vehicle to the applicant. He has not complied with those orders. Rather, he now gives evidence that he sold the vehicle for $23,500. His evidence is unreliable, unsafe, and implausible. He says that he sold the vehicle to Mr P. He does not adduce evidence from Mr P. He produces no sale documents, no receipt, no transfer of registration documents, and no documents that might evidence transfers of money. It is open for me to find that such corroborative evidence was reasonably available to the respondent but that he gives no explanation for not adducing the evidence. It is open for me, therefore, to infer that the bringing of such evidence would not have assisted the respondent’s case.[3]
Further, where I accept the evidence of the applicant over that of the respondent, there is evidence to corroborate her claim that the respondent remains in possession or control of the vehicle. She took a photograph of the respondent driving the same vehicle outside her house after he claims to have disposed of it. She has received notices of fines for unpaid road tolls also after the claimed sale of the vehicle. The respondent gives no persuasive explanation for his failure to adhere to court orders for him to hand the vehicle to the applicant.
Consequently, I find that Motor Vehicle 1 remains in the possession or control of the respondent. It has a value according to the applicant’s case summary of $18,000. The property pool is otherwise settled.

CONTRIBUTIONS

The parties commenced cohabitation in January 2010. There is no evidence of either party having any substantial wealth at that time.
The respondent was generally self-employed for a majority of the relationship. The evidence of the financial history suggests he was a hard worker and good provider for his family.
The applicant was initially employed full-time. She later worked in the parties’ joint hospitality enterprise. Prominently though there are two children of the relationship it is clear that the applicant assumed the role of primary parent and homemaker.
From the evidence available to me I am satisfied generally that the parties’ contributions were equal until their separation.
I am satisfied that the applicant has made far superior contributions than the respondent post-separation where she has effectively had the sole care of the two young children. This has impacted on her ability to return full-time to the workforce. She has been the sole financial provider for the children. The respondent pays no child support for the children. I reject the uncorroborated assertions of the respondent that he has made large financial cash contributions to the applicant. Such assertions are not corroborated by any documentation. The assertions sits uncomfortably with the history of family violence perpetrated by the respondent to the applicant. Such assertions are not evidenced in any of the applicant’s own overt financial circumstances.
I am easily satisfied that the applicant’s contributions have been made more arduous and onerous by way of the respondent’s conduct and the perpetration of family violence. The applicant’s affidavit is replete with a highly particularised history of violence of various types. They are in a large part unchallenged given the inability of the respondent to cross-examine. Nevertheless, there is corroborative and empirical evidence to support the applicant’s assertions. There are numerous family violence orders made in her favour. There is evidence of breach of those orders. There is evidence of breach of orders of this Court and notably by the failure of the respondent to comply with an order to hand Motor Vehicle 1 to the applicant. There is evidence of the applicant taking refuge in a safe house at a women’s shelter for a period of some nine months.
I am satisfied that the family violence perpetrated on her has impacted on the applicant’s ability to care and support the children. The violence has been coercive and controlling. The violence has been physical. The violence has been of a financial type.
The parties separated effectively in 2019 except for a short reconciliation in 2021 and I accept the applicant’s contributions over the six subsequent years have been significant and must be seen against the relatively small value of the property pool available to the Court for adjustment. That is, I am of the view that any adjustment contribution should be reality tested against the value of that pool.
In all the circumstances and with weighty evidence of the significantly greater post separation financial contributions of the applicant over a period of some six years then I am of the view that an adjustment of 20 per cent of the property pool to her is appropriate, just and equitable.

SECTION 90SM(5) CONSIDERATIONS

Prominently, an issue for consideration here is the asserted retention by the respondent of assets or the proceeds of sale of assets to the exclusion of the applicant thereby denying their inclusion in the property pool. Again, where there are issues of credit and disputed fact between the parties, I generally prefer the evidence of the applicant which is in the whole more consistent, particularised, and informed than that of the respondent who in the main presents simple blanket denials.
The property at Property 2B Street was purchased by the respondent in 2013 and sold by him in mid-2021. The respondent received the totality of the sale price proceeds as evidenced by the Q Bank statement being a total of $287,722. Documents disclose withdrawals by the respondent to M Financial Services of $122,848 on 13 July 2021. Further, $50,000 was attributed to the purchase of Motor Vehicle 3. A further $50,000 was paid to a Mr O. The balance proceeds of some $65,000 remain unaccounted for. The respondent adduced no evidence from or in respect of Mr O. As such, I find that he has retained for his own benefit or has not accounted to the applicant for E$115,000 in respect of the proceeds of sale of property 2B Street.
Pursuant to court orders the property at Suburb E was sold in mid-2023 for $581,000. Contrary to court orders the respondent retained the deposit of $17,014.
The Suburb G property was purchased in early 2019 for $1,700,000 and sold by the applicant on court orders in early 2024 for $1,800,000. In this respect I reject the respondent’s claim of a potential purchasers for $5 million to be uncorroborated and fanciful and further an example of the respondent’s credit being compromised in these proceedings.
I am satisfied generally that the respondent failed to vacate the property leaving it in an unfit condition. I have no evidence, however, of any impact on the sale price and cannot, therefore, make any specific findings of impact of the respondent’s behaviour.
I accept that during the relationship the parties loaned E$100,000 to the respondent’s brother, Mr N Shahrukh. The subpoenaed bank statements persuade me that Mr N repaid the loan. I accept the applicant’s evidence that she has not benefited from these monies. The respondent’s evidence contradicting the fact of repayment of the loan is unconvincing. Again, the respondent did not adduce evidence from his brother, Mr N. He gives no explanation for not adducing evidence from his brother. I am further satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the respondent retained the benefit of those monies in a sum of at least $95,000 and perhaps of a greater amount. He has given no explanation as to the disbursement of those funds.
The respondent purchased Motor Vehicle 3 from the proceeds of sale of property 2B Street. The bank records show a payment of $50,000. The applicant says that the purchase price was $70,000 with an additional $20,000 paid by cash.
The respondent says that the vehicle was sold for $57,000. Again, there are no documents to evidence the sale. The respondent does not give evidence as to the disbursement of the sale proceeds. The applicant says that the respondent retains the vehicle. There is a photograph of a motor vehicle3 taken on the Suburb G property in March 2020. In any event, it is clear that the respondent either retains possession and control of the vehicle or has benefited from the $57,000 sale proceeds.
Orders for partial property settlement were made throughout these proceedings and benefiting both parties. Specifically, orders in respect of the applicant’s receipt of $110,000 were to be “categorised at trial”. That advancement must now necessarily be considered under s 90SM(5). I am satisfied that the applicant has not personally received those monies, but they have been utilised in prior legal costs.
In his final submission, counsel for the respondent conceded generally that the respondent has paid $220,000 in legal costs. These concessions if accepted give some explanation to the otherwise unexplained retention of monies by the respondent. In any event, it is clear, on any version of the evidence, that the respondent has retained monies/assets/paid legal costs of perhaps double that received by the applicant.
The applicant had accrued superannuation entitlements of E$23,000. She has cashed in these monies and presumably under hardship provisions. My findings above suggests clearly that those monies were used for reasonable costs of living for the applicant and the children where she received no financial support from the respondent. I note for example that the applicant was required to purchase herself a motor vehicle following separation.
Generally, it is relevant to note that the children remain in the sole care of the respondent. They are still young. She is likely to retain that sole care status or at least a primary carer status until they are 18 years old. She receives no financial support from the respondent. On his presentation and his evidence in court, I cannot be confident that the respondent will contribute to the children’s financial needs and hence this burden will fall heavily on the applicant.
The applicant works casually in hospitality. She works to her ability given her obligations for the children. The respondent says he is now a student studying. Presumably he will complete this course and enter remunerative employment or, perhaps given his history, self-employment. He does, however, have the capacity for alternative employment or self-employment as a tradesman. He presents as a resourceful and historically hard-working young man.
The applicant’s trial affidavit is comprehensive and highly particularised in respect of allegations of family violence perpetrated by the respondent. I have set out my findings of family violence above. Relevant to the s 90SM(5) considerations I am satisfied that the applicant was the victim of concerted, consistent and various types of family violence at the hands of the respondent and that this impact remains in respect of her current and future circumstances not least being her sole care of the children and their financial support. I note that she continues to receive psychological assistance. I find that there should be a significant adjustment to the applicant on account of the s 90SM(5) considerations.

CONCLUSION

After finding that there should be an adjustment after contributions to the applicant of 20 per cent of the limited property pool with the inclusion of the motor vehicle 1 into that pool, I am easily satisfied that there should be a further significant adjustment to the applicant after consideration of the relevant s 90SM(5) factors I find that the respondent has retained assets or monies far above those retained by the applicant which effectively is the partial property settlement utilised for her legal costs. I find that the respondent has perpetrated family violence to a high degree. I find that he has absolved himself of financial support for the children.
Significantly, where the property pool here is of minimal dollar value, I must give a sense of reality to the consideration of any adjustment under s 90SM(5).[4] Even a 30 per cent adjustment gives the applicant little actual dollar value. Consequently, and given the limitations under the legislation together with my findings, it is irresistible that I should order the applicant to receive the totality of the property pool set out above and now inclusive of the motor vehicle 1. Specifically, however, and in summary, I have found the respondent to have had use of the following:
(a) Proceeds of repaid loan from his brother, Mr N E$95,000.

(b) Unaccounted proceeds of sale of property 2B Street E$115,000.

(c) Deposit on Suburb E Sale contract $17,014.

(d) Motor Vehicle 3 or sale proceeds $57,000.

(e) Motor Vehicle 1 or sale proceeds (but now

to be transferred to the applicant) $18,000.

The applicant asks for specific orders in relation to Motor Vehicle 1 including the intervention of the Marshall. While the respondent’s evidence suggests such a course to be inevitable, I will first give him the opportunity to voluntarily hand that vehicle to the applicant while noting, of course, his non-compliance with two similar orders. There will be a default provision in the terms sought by the applicant.
Given the limited value of the “existing” property pool and with considerable weight on the far superior contributions of the applicant post-separation with findings justifying a further significant adjustment to the applicant after consideration of the s 90SM(5) factors I am satisfied that orders in such terms provide justice and equity.
